If $b_{i}$ has a log-gamma distribution (or $e^{b_{i}}$ has a gamma distribution), we have a Poisson-gamma model. In Section 4.5 we showed that the marginal distribution of the Poisson-gamma $y_{i}$ is the negative binomial, so it is possible to get an explicit marginal likelihood of the fixed effects parameter.

In Sections 4.2, 4.5 and 6.5 we have actually discussed the binomial and Poisson models with overdispersion. The new feature in GLMM is that we try to put more structure on the overdispersion pattern. If the random effects parameters are of interest, the GLMM specification is essential.

The simple mean model in the previous examples can be extended to a general regression of the form

$$
h\left(\mu_{i}\right)=x_{i}^{\prime} \beta+b_{i},
$$

where $h(\cdot)$ is a link function. In the examples, the germination data may come from an experiment with several treatment variables, or we may want to associate family size with socio-demographic variables. If the interest is on the fixed parameter $\beta$, the role of a random parameter $b_{i}$ is to cover for some unmeasured variables, so that, conditional on $b_{i}$, the outcome $y_{i}$ has a recognized distribution with mean $\mu_{i}$, and we get a better inference for $\beta$.

## General specification

The GLM part of GLMM specifies that conditional on $b$ the outcome $y_{i}$ is independently distributed according to an exponential family model (Section 6.5) of the form

$$
\log p\left(y_{i} \mid b\right)=\frac{y_{i} \psi_{i}-A\left(\psi_{i}\right)}{\phi}+c\left(y_{i}, \phi\right)
$$

Let $\mu_{i} \equiv E\left(y_{i} \mid b\right)$ and assume that

$$
h\left(\mu_{i}\right)=x_{i}^{\prime} \beta+z_{i}^{\prime} b .
$$

Writing $\mu$ as the vector of $\mu_{i}$ 's and thinking of $h(\cdot)$ as a function that applies element by element, we can write the model more concisely as

$$
h(\mu)=X \beta+Z b
$$

As described in Section 6.5 this model specification is equivalent to assuming particular mean and variance functions. That is, conditional on the
random effects $b$, the outcome $y_{i}$ is independently distributed with mean $\mu_{i}$ and variance $\phi v_{i}\left(\mu_{i}\right)$.

We complete the model specification by making an assumption on the random effects $b$. In general $b$ does not have to be normally distributed, though such a distribution usually leads to simple computational formulae. Let $\theta$ include the dispersion parameter $\phi$ above and any other parameters needed to specify $b$.

The marginal variance of $y_{i}$ is

$$
\begin{aligned}
\operatorname{var}\left(y_{i}\right) & =E\left\{\operatorname{var}\left(y_{i} \mid b\right)\right\}+\operatorname{var}\left\{E\left(y_{i} \mid b\right)\right\} \\
& =\phi E\left\{v_{i}\left(\mu_{i}\right)\right\}+\operatorname{var}\left(\mu_{i}\right) .
\end{aligned}
$$

Overdispersion might come from both terms on the right-hand side; the first term accounts for the contribution of the dispersion family, and the second term is the contribution of the random effects.

Example 17.4: Suppose, conditional on (scalar) $b$, the (scalar) outcome $y$ has mean $\mu$ and variance $\phi \mu$, where

$$
\log \mu=\beta_{0}+b
$$

and $e^{b}$ is gamma with mean one and variance $\nu$. The marginal mean of $y$ is $E y=e^{\beta_{0}}$, and the marginal variance of $y$ is

$$
\begin{aligned}
\operatorname{var}(y) & =\phi e^{\beta_{0}}+\nu e^{2 \beta_{0}} \\
& =\phi E y+\nu(E y)^{2} .
\end{aligned}
$$

If $\phi=1$ we have the Poisson-gamma or negative binomial model; if $\nu=0$ we have the dispersion-family Poisson model. Hence the GLMM can fit potentially richer overdispersion patterns. In recent applications of GLMM, except in normal models, the parameter $\phi$ is usually set to one. Lee and Nelder (2000) give some applications that use the full model.

Example 17.5: Classical animal breeding programmes are usually limited to continuous outcomes (such as meat or milk yields) which are assumed normal, but there are increasingly many nonnormal characteristics that require GLMM techniques, for example calving difficulty in cows, ability to produce twins in sheep, disease resistance, fertility rate, performance of race horses, etc. The model in each case is usually of the form

$$
h(\mu)=X \beta+Z b
$$

where the parameter of interest is the random parameter $b$, the vector genetic merit of the animals under observation. The fixed parameter $\beta$ accounts for predictors such as age of animal, farm characteristics, etc. Applications in animal breeding typically involve thousands of related animals (see Section 17.3), demanding highly nontrivial computations.

### 17.9 Exact likelihood in GLMM

While the marginal likelihood in the normal-normal GLMM is relatively simple (Section 17.2), the general case in notoriously difficult. The approx-
imate computations will be described in the next section, and here we illustrate the computation of the exact marginal likelihood for the binomialnormal model. Suppose, conditional on $b_{i}$, the observed $y_{i}$ is binomial with mean $\mu_{i}=n_{i} p_{i}$, where

$$
\operatorname{logit} p_{i}=x_{i}^{\prime} \beta+b_{i},
$$

and $b_{i}$ is a random sample from $N\left(0, \sigma^{2}\right)$.
The marginal probability of $y_{i}$ is

$$
\begin{aligned}
P\left(y_{i}=k\right) & =E\left\{P\left(y_{i}=k \mid b_{i}\right)\right\} \\
& =\int\binom{n_{i}}{k} p^{k}(1-p)^{n_{i}-k} \phi(b / \sigma) d b / \sigma \\
& \equiv p\left(n_{i}, k, \beta, \sigma^{2}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

where logit $p=x_{i}^{\prime} \beta+b$ and $\phi(u)$ is the standard normal density. Given the observed $y_{i}$ 's, the marginal log-likelihood of the fixed parameters is

$$
\log L\left(\beta, \sigma^{2}\right)=\sum_{i} \log p\left(n_{i}, y_{i}, \beta, \sigma^{2}\right) .
$$

If $n_{i} \equiv n$, the likelihood reduces to

$$
\log L\left(\beta, \sigma^{2}\right)=\sum_{k} m_{k} \log p\left(n, k, \beta, \sigma^{2}\right),
$$

where $m_{k}$ is the number of $y_{i}$ 's equal to $k$.
This illustrates the computational problem of exact likelihood inference in GLMM: in general there is no closed form solution for integrating out the random effects $b$. In practice it has to be computed numerically, for example using the Gaussian quadrature technique. This is feasible if the random effects are independent, so we only need to evaluate single integrals. If the random effects are correlated, the integrals become multidimensional, and the exact approach is no longer tractable.

Example 17.6: We return to the germination data analysed in Example 6.4. The data were from a $2 \times 2$ factorial experiment of seed variety and type of root extract. For comparison Table 17.3 shows again the summary result using the ordinary logistic model

$$
\operatorname{logit} p_{i}=x_{i}^{\prime} \beta \text {. }
$$

In particular the result shows a significant interaction term.
The deviance of this fixed effects model is 33.28 with $21-4=17$ degrees of freedom; for binomial data this can be interpreted as a goodness-of-fit statistic, indicating overdispersion ( P -value $=0.01$ ). Now consider a mixed model that accounts for the plate variability:

$$
\operatorname{logit} p_{i}=x_{i}^{\prime} \beta+b_{i},
$$

where the plate effects $b_{i}$ 's are iid $N\left(0, \sigma_{b}^{2}\right)$. The results of the numerical optimization of the exact binomial-normal log-likelihood are shown in Table 17.3. The Gaussian quadrature method is used to compute the integral. The parameter estimates from the two models are comparable, but, as expected from an

|  | Ordinary |  | Mixed model |  |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Effects | Estimate | se | Estimate | se |
| Constant | -0.56 | 0.13 | -0.55 | 0.17 |
| Seed variety | 0.15 | 0.22 | 0.10 | 0.28 |
| Root extract | 1.32 | 0.18 | 1.34 | 0.24 |
| Interaction | -0.78 | 0.31 | -0.81 | 0.38 |
| Plate $\sigma_{b}$ |  |  | 0.24 | 0.11 |

Table 17.3: Summary analysis of germination data using the ordinary logistic regression and the exact binomial-normal mixed model.

overdispersed model, all of the standard errors from the mixed model are slightly larger.

The MLE of $\sigma_{b}^{2}$ is $\widehat{\sigma}_{b}^{2}=0.056$ (se $=0.052$ ), but the standard error is not meaningful since the likelihood is not regular. Figure 17.3 shows the exact profile $\log$-likelihood of $\sigma_{b}^{2}, \sigma_{b}$ and $\log \sigma_{b}$ with their quadratic approximations. The best is for $\sigma_{b}$, except near zero. The exact likelihood interval for $\sigma_{b}$ at $15 \%$ cutoff covers zero; the likelihood of $H: \sigma_{b}=0$ is around $31 \%$, indicating that there is no evidence against the ordinary logistic model. The Wald statistic based on $\widehat{\sigma}_{b}$ is $z=0.24 / 0.11=2.18$.

The exact likelihood result is in conflict with the earlier goodness-of-fit test that shows evidence against the ordinary logistic regression. (An exact version of the $\chi^{2}$ goodness-of-fit test is also significant; see Exercise 17.4.) In this case, because it is slightly more conservative, we may want to keep the binomial-normal model, although both models lead to similar conclusions.

### 17.10 Approximate likelihood in GLMM

The basis of the likelihood approximation in GLMM is the extended likelihood that includes the random effects, plus the heuristics provided by the normal mixed models. Given the standard setup of GLMM in the last section, the joint likelihood of the parameters is

$$
\log L(\beta, \theta, b)=\log p(y \mid b)+\log p(b)
$$

where $p(y \mid b)$ is the likelihood based on the conditional distibution of $y$ given $b$, and $p(b)$ is the likelihood based on the assumed distribution of the random effects. Lee and Nelder (1996) refer to this as the hierarchical likelihood or h-likelihood.

Example 17.7: For the binomial-normal model, conditional on $b$, let $y_{i}$ be $\operatorname{binomial}\left(n_{i}, p_{i}\right)$ where

$$
\operatorname{logit} p_{i}=x_{i}^{\prime} \beta+z_{i}^{\prime} b \text {. }
$$

Assume that the random effects $b_{i}$ 's are iid $N\left(0, \sigma^{2}\right)$; it is obvious how to modify the likelihood if $b$ is multivariate normal. Given the data $y_{1}, \ldots, y_{n}$ we obtain

$$
\log L(\beta, \theta, b)=\sum\left\{y_{i} \log p_{i}+\left(n_{i}-y_{i}\right) \log \left(1-p_{i}\right)\right\}-\frac{1}{2 \sigma^{2}} \sum_{i} b_{i}^{2},
$$

where $p_{i}$ is understood to be a function of $\beta$ and $b$, and $\theta \equiv \sigma^{2}$. For a fixed value of $\sigma^{2}$ this likelihood is trivial to compute or optimize.

![](https://cdn.mathpix.com/cropped/b2d32ae7-3d92-44a8-9e55-6a263ca8ddd8-478.jpg?height=899&width=1122&top_left_y=187&top_left_x=114)
Figure 17.3: Exact profile log-likelihoods (solid lines) of $\sigma_{b}^{2}, \sigma_{b}$ and $\log \sigma_{b}$, and their quadratic approximations.

Example 17.8: Suppose conditional on $b$ the outcome $y_{i}$ is Poisson with mean $\mu_{i}$, where

$$
\log \mu_{i}=x_{i}^{\prime} \beta+b_{i}
$$

and $u_{i}=e^{b_{i}}$ is iid gamma with mean 1 and shape parameter $\alpha$. Given $y_{1}, \ldots, y_{n}$ we have

$$
\log L(\beta, \theta, b)=\sum_{i}\left(-\mu_{i}+y_{i} \log \mu_{i}\right)+\sum_{i}\left\{\alpha b_{i}-\alpha e^{b_{i}}+\alpha \log \alpha-\log \Gamma(\alpha)\right\},
$$

where $\theta \equiv \alpha$.
The joint likelihood $L(\beta, \theta, b)$ is generally much easier to evaluate and optimize than the marginal likelihood $L(\beta, \theta)$. For the normal model in Section 17.4 it is shown that, at fixed value of $\theta$, the estimate $\widehat{\beta}$ from $L(\beta, \theta, b)$ is the same as that from the marginal likelihood. The Poissongamma model above is another case where the joint likelihood estimate of $\widehat{\beta}$ is the same as the marginal likelihood estimate. Breslow and Clayton (1993) present applications that support the practical viability of such an approach. Lee and Nelder (1996) show that under fairly general conditions the two estimates are asymptotically close.

## Estimating $\beta$ and $b$ given $\theta$

Given a fixed value of $\theta$ we can use a quadratic approximation of the likelihood to derive an IWLS algorithm; see Section 6.7. Let the outcome vector $y$ have mean $\mu$ and

$$
h(\mu)=X \beta+Z b .
$$

Given initial values $\beta^{0}$ and $b^{0}$, the exponential family log-likelihood can be approximated by

$$
-\frac{1}{2} \log |\Sigma|-\frac{1}{2}(Y-X \beta-Z b)^{\prime} \Sigma^{-1}(Y-X \beta-Z b),
$$

where $Y$ is a working vector with elements

$$
Y_{i}=x_{i}^{\prime} \beta^{0}+z_{i}^{\prime} b^{0}+\frac{\partial h}{\partial \mu_{i}}\left(y_{i}-\mu_{i}^{0}\right)
$$

and $\Sigma$ is a diagonal matrix of the variance of the working vector with elements

$$
\Sigma_{i i}=\left(\frac{\partial h}{\partial \mu_{i}}\right)^{2} \phi v_{i}\left(\mu_{i}^{0}\right),
$$

where $\phi v_{i}\left(\mu_{i}^{0}\right)$ is the conditional variance of $y_{i}$ given $b$. The derivative $\partial h / \partial \mu_{i}$ is also evaluated at the current values of $\beta$ and $b$.

If the random effects parameter $b$ is assumed normal with mean zero and variance $D=D(\theta)$, we have a familiar normal-based formula:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log L(\beta, \theta, b) \approx & -\frac{1}{2} \log |\Sigma|-\frac{1}{2}(Y-X \beta-Z b)^{\prime} \Sigma^{-1}(Y-X \beta-Z b) \\
& -\frac{1}{2} \log |D|-\frac{1}{2} b^{\prime} D^{-1} b .
\end{aligned}
$$

This yields the usual mixed model equations to update $\beta$ and $b$ :

$$
\left(\begin{array}{cc}
X^{\prime} \Sigma^{-1} X & X^{\prime} \Sigma^{-1} Z \\
Z^{\prime} \Sigma^{-1} X & Z^{\prime} \Sigma^{-1} Z+D^{-1}
\end{array}\right)\binom{\beta}{b}=\binom{X^{\prime} \Sigma^{-1} Y}{Z^{\prime} \Sigma^{-1} Y} .
$$

An iterative backfitting algorithm (Section 17.4) might be used to find the solution. The iteration continues by recomputing $Y$ and $\Sigma$. Hence the computation of estimates in GLMM involves repeated applications of normal-based formulae.

## Nonnormal random effects*

The estimation of nonnormal random effects is best derived on a case-by-case basis, as different models may have different optimal algorithms. However, as an illustration of the additional work needed, we show here a useful general technique.

If $b$ is not normally distributed an extra step is needed first to approximate its log-likelihood by a quadratic form. Let $b_{i}$ 's be an iid sample from $p(\cdot)$, and let $\ell\left(b_{i}\right)=\log p\left(b_{i}\right)$. Using initial value $b_{i}^{0}$

$$
\log p\left(b_{i}\right) \approx \log p\left(b_{i}^{c}\right)+\frac{1}{2} \ell^{\prime \prime}\left(b_{i}^{0}\right)\left(b_{i}-b_{i}^{c}\right)^{2},
$$

where

$$
b_{i}^{c}=b_{i}^{0}-\frac{\ell^{\prime}\left(b_{i}^{0}\right)}{\ell^{\prime \prime}\left(b_{i}^{0}\right)}
$$

Now let $D^{-1}=\operatorname{diag}\left[-\ell^{\prime \prime}\left(b_{i}^{0}\right)\right]$, the Fisher information matrix of $b$ based on $p(\cdot)$, and let $\ell^{\prime}\left(b^{0}\right)$ be the vector of $\ell^{\prime}\left(b_{i}^{0}\right)$, so in vector notation

$$
b^{c}=b^{0}+D \ell^{\prime}\left(b^{0}\right)
$$

We can now write

$$
\log p(b)=\log p\left(b^{c}\right)-\frac{1}{2}\left(b-b^{c}\right)^{\prime} D^{-1}\left(b-b^{c}\right) .
$$

In the normal case $D$ is the covariance matrix, and $b^{c}=0$.
After combining this with the quadratic approximation of $\log p(y \mid b)$, we take the derivatives with respect to $\beta$ and $b$ and find the updating equation

$$
\left(\begin{array}{cc}
X^{\prime} \Sigma^{-1} X & X^{\prime} \Sigma^{-1} Z \\
Z^{\prime} \Sigma^{-1} X & Z^{\prime} \Sigma^{-1} Z+D^{-1}
\end{array}\right)\binom{\beta}{b}=\binom{X^{\prime} \Sigma^{-1} Y}{Z^{\prime} \Sigma^{-1} Y+D^{-1} b^{c}} .
$$

This is similar to (17.25), except for the term $D^{-1} b^{c}$.
Example 17.9: If $e^{b_{i}}$ is iid gamma with mean 1 and shape parameter $\alpha$,

$$
\ell\left(b_{i}\right)=\alpha b_{i}-\alpha e^{b_{i}}+\alpha \log \alpha-\log \Gamma(\alpha)
$$

and we have

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ell^{\prime}\left(b_{i}\right) & =\alpha-\alpha e^{b_{i}} \\
\ell^{\prime \prime}\left(b_{i}\right) & =-\alpha e^{b_{i}}
\end{aligned}
$$

so, using the starting value $b^{0}$, we get $D^{-1}=\operatorname{diag}\left[\alpha e^{b^{0}}\right]$ and

$$
\begin{aligned}
b^{c} & =b^{0}+\frac{\alpha-\alpha e^{b^{0}}}{\alpha e^{b^{0}}} \\
& =b^{0}+e^{-b^{0}}-1
\end{aligned}
$$

Example 17.10: Suppose $b_{i}$ is iid Cauchy with location 0 and scale $\sigma_{b}$, so

$$
\ell\left(b_{i}\right)=-\log \left(1+\frac{b_{i}^{2}}{\sigma_{b}^{2}}\right)
$$

and we have

$$
\ell^{\prime}\left(b_{i}\right)=-\frac{2 b_{i}}{b_{i}^{2}+\sigma_{b}^{2}}
$$



$$
\ell^{\prime \prime}\left(b_{i}\right)=-\frac{2\left(b_{i}^{2}-\sigma_{b}^{2}\right)}{\left(b_{i}^{2}+\sigma_{b}^{2}\right)^{2}}
$$

and $D^{-1}=\operatorname{diag}\left[-\ell^{\prime \prime}\left(b^{0}\right)\right]$ and

$$
\begin{aligned}
b^{c} & =b^{0}-b^{0} \frac{\left(b^{0}\right)^{2}+\sigma_{b}^{2}}{\left(b^{0}\right)^{2}-\sigma_{b}^{2}} \\
& =-\frac{2 \sigma_{b}^{2} b^{0}}{\left(b^{0}\right)^{2}-\sigma_{b}^{2}}
\end{aligned}
$$

In this Cauchy example we can derive another computational scheme. First note that

$$
\ell^{\prime}(b)=-D^{-1} b
$$

where now $D^{-1} \equiv \operatorname{diag}\left[2 /\left(b^{2}+\sigma_{b}^{2}\right)\right]$. Combining this with the derivative of (17.23) with respect to $b$, we can update $b$ using

$$
\left(Z^{\prime} \Sigma^{-1} Z+D^{-1}\right) b=Z^{\prime} \Sigma^{-1}(Y-X \beta),
$$

where $D$ is computed at the starting value $b^{0}$. This is simpler and probably more stable than the general method above.

## Estimating $\theta$

Even in the normal case, the estimate of the variance parameter cannot be computed from the joint likelihood $L(\beta, b, \theta)$. Instead, we have shown in Section 17.5 that the profile likelihood of $\theta$ is equivalent to a modified profile from the joint likelihood:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\log L(\theta) & =\log L(\widehat{\beta}, \theta) \\
& =\log L(\widehat{\beta}, \theta, \widehat{b})-\frac{1}{2} \log \left|Z^{\prime} \Sigma^{-1} Z+D^{-1}\right|
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\theta$ enters the function through $\Sigma, D, \widehat{\beta}$ and $\widehat{b}$.
The approximate methods of variance component estimation in GLMM have not yet settled to an agreed form, but a strong candidate is to estimate $\theta$ by maximizing a modified profile likelihood exactly as in the normal case (Lee and Nelder 1996):

$$
\log L(\theta)=\log L(\widehat{\beta}, \theta, \widehat{b})-\frac{1}{2} \log \left|Z^{\prime} \Sigma^{-1} Z+D^{-1}\right|
$$

where $\widehat{\beta}$ and $\widehat{b}$ are computed at fixed $\theta$ as described before. There is one crucial difference: in the normal case $\Sigma$ is typically a simple function of a variance component, but in GLMM $\Sigma$ is also a function of the unknown mean $\mu$, and hence of $\beta$ and $b$. Since $\mu$ is unknown it will be convenient to compute $\Sigma$ using $\widehat{\beta}$ and $\widehat{b}$.

A heuristic justification in terms of Laplace's integral approximation (Section 10.6) is given by Breslow and Clayton (1993). It has been shown in many examples that this method does provide solutions which are close to the exact marginal likelihood estimates, provided the variance component $\theta$ is not too large. The method tends to underestimate $\theta$, and the problem can be severe for large $\theta$.

## Joint estimation of $\beta, \theta$ and $b$

As in the normal case, the approximate MLEs of $\beta, \theta$ and $b$ are the joint maximizers of

$$
Q(\beta, \theta, b)=\log L(\beta, \theta, b)-\frac{1}{2} \log \left|Z^{\prime} \Sigma^{-1} Z+D^{-1}\right| .
$$

To derive an iterative estimation procedure the first term is approximated by a quadratic form (17.24). However, in contrast with the normal mixed models, because of the dependence of $\Sigma$ on $\beta$ and $b$, we cannot immediately justify this iterative algorithm:

1. Compute $\widehat{\beta}$ and $\widehat{b}$ given $\theta$ by solving (17.25).
2. Fixing $\beta$ and $b$ at the values $\widehat{\beta}$ and $\widehat{b}$, update $\theta$ by maximizing $Q$.
3. Iterate between 1 and 2 until convergence.

The algorithm is appropriate, for example, if $\Sigma$ is a slowly varying function of $\mu$. This means we can ignore the derivative of the second term of $Q$ with respect to $\beta$ and $b$, so the first step is justified. This is not the only way to estimate $\theta$; any derivative-free method can be used to optimize $\log L(\theta)$ in (17.27) directly.

In the important special case where $\phi=1$, and

- $\Sigma$ is assumed to be a slowly varying function of the mean $\mu$, and
- $b$ is normal with mean zero and variance $\sigma_{b}^{2} R$, where $R$ is a known matrix of rank $q$, so $\theta \equiv \sigma_{b}^{2}$,
we can derive an update formula for Step 2 of the algorithm as in Section 17.5:

$$
\sigma_{b}^{2}=\frac{1}{q}\left[b^{\prime} R^{-1} b+\operatorname{trace}\left\{\left(Z^{\prime} \Sigma^{-1} Z+\sigma_{b}^{-2} R^{-1}\right)^{-1} R^{-1}\right\}\right]
$$

where all unknown quantities on the right-hand side are evaluated at the last available values.

Computation in the general case $\phi \neq 1$ can also follow the algorithm presented in Section 17.5 if we are willing to make the quadratic approximation (17.23) around the estimates $\widehat{\beta}$ and $\widehat{b}$ as a profile log-likelihood for $\phi$. Alternatively we can use the extended quasi-likelihood approximation (4.5) for an explicit profile likelihood of $\phi$.

## Approximate inference

The simplest inference on $\beta$ and $b$ can be provided by computing the standard errors from the Fisher information matrix. By analogy with the normal model, given variance parameter $\theta$, the Fisher information for $\beta$ is

$$
I(\widehat{\beta})=X^{\prime} V^{-1} X
$$

where $V=\Sigma+Z D Z^{\prime}$, and the Fisher information for $b$ is

$$
I(\widehat{b})=\left(Z^{\prime} \Sigma^{-1} Z+D^{-1}\right)
$$

In practice these are evaluated at the estimated value of $\theta$. These formulae imply (i) the inference on $\widehat{\beta}$ accounts for the fact that $b$, but not $\theta$, is unknown; (ii) inference on $\widehat{b}$ does not account for the fact that both $\beta$ and $\theta$ are estimated.

Standard errors of $\hat{\theta}$ can be computed by taking derivatives of $\log L(\theta)$ in (17.27), but bear in mind that the likelihood function may not be regular.
Example 17.11: We apply the methodology to Crowder's data in Example 17.6. Recall that $y_{i}$ is binomial $\left(n_{i}, p_{i}\right)$ with

$$
\operatorname{logit} p_{i}=x_{i}^{\prime} \beta+b_{i},
$$

so the design matrix $Z$ is an identity matrix $I_{21}$, and $b$ is normal with mean zero and variance $\sigma_{b}^{2} I_{21}$.

We can use the ordinary GLM estimates as the starting value for $\beta$, and set $b=0$ and $\sigma_{b}^{2}=0.01$. Then:

1. Update $b$ by solving

$$
\left(Z^{\prime} \Sigma^{-1} Z+\sigma_{b}^{-2} I_{q}\right) b=Z^{\prime} \Sigma^{-1}(Y-X \beta) .
$$

This reduces to

$$
b_{i}=\frac{\Sigma_{i i}^{-1}\left(Y_{i}-x_{i}^{\prime} \beta\right)}{\Sigma_{i i}^{-1}+\sigma_{b}^{-2}}
$$

where $Y$ is the working vector with elements

$$
Y_{i}=x_{i}^{\prime} \beta+b_{i}+\frac{y_{i}-n_{i} p_{i}}{n_{i} p_{i}\left(1-p_{i}\right)}
$$

and $p_{i}$ is computed from

$$
\operatorname{logit} p_{i}=x_{i}^{\prime} \beta+b_{i} .
$$

The matrix $\Sigma$ is diagonal with elements

$$
\Sigma_{i i}=\frac{1}{n_{i} p_{i}\left(1-p_{i}\right)}
$$

2. Update $\beta$ by solving

$$
\left(X^{\prime} \Sigma^{-1} X\right) \beta=X^{\prime} \Sigma^{-1}(Y-b)
$$

after recomputing $Y$ and $\Sigma$.
3. Update $\sigma_{b}^{2}$ using

$$
\sigma_{b}^{2}=\frac{1}{21}\left[b^{\prime} b+\operatorname{trace}\left\{\left(\Sigma^{-1}+\sigma_{b}^{-2} I_{21}\right)^{-1}\right\}\right] .
$$

4. Iterate 1, 2 and 3 until convergence.

The results at convergence are shown in Table 17.4 under the 'Approximate' columns. For comparison the ordinary logistic regression and the 'Exact'

|  | Ordinary |  | Mixed models |  |  |  |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
|  | Exact |  | Approximate |  |  |  |
| Effects | Estimate | se | Estimate | se | Estimate | se |
| Constant | -0.56 | 0.13 | -0.55 | 0.17 | -0.54 | 0.17 |
| Seed variety | 0.15 | 0.22 | 0.10 | 0.28 | 0.10 | 0.27 |
| Root extract | 1.32 | 0.18 | 1.34 | 0.24 | 1.33 | 0.23 |
| Interaction | -0.78 | 0.31 | -0.81 | 0.38 | -0.80 | 0.38 |
| Plate $\sigma_{b}$ |  |  | 0.24 | 0.11 | 0.23 | 0.11 |

binomial-normal mixed model results from Table 17.3 are also shown. In this example the approximate method yields results very close to the exact results.

To show the quality of the likelihood approximation, Figure 17.4 compares the approximate profile likelihood of $\sigma_{b}$ computed according to (17.27) and its exact version computed in the previous section. $\square$

### 17.11 Exercises

Exercise 17.1: As stated after equation (17.2), show that the MLE of $\mu_{i}$ is $\bar{y}_{i}$ regardless of any constraint on the $b_{i}$ 's.
Exercise 17.2: Show that $\widehat{b}_{i}$ in (17.3) is the conditional mean of the distribution of $b_{i}$ given $y$.
Exercise 17.3: Show that the solution of the mixed model equation (17.12) is exactly the same as (17.7) and (17.10).
Exercise 17.4: Compute the exact distribution of the $\chi^{2}$ goodness-of-fit test for the germination data in Example 17.6. (Hint: think of the success-failure data from each group as a $k \times 2$ table. If the binomial model is correct, the
distribution of the table entries conditional on the observed margins is hypergeometric; recall Fisher's exact test for the $2 \times 2$ table. Hence, we can generate realizations according to the hypergeometric model, compute a $\chi^{2}$ statistic for each realization, and then repeat a large number of times.)
Exercise 17.5: Figure 17.5 shows the data from a clinical trial of an epileptic drug progabide (Thall and Vail, 1990). Patients were randomized to the active $(\operatorname{Tr}=1)$ or placebo $(\operatorname{Tr}=0)$ drug in addition to a standard treatment. The follow-up data $y_{1}$ to $y_{4}$ are the number of seizures during the two-week period prior to each of four visits.
